heroOverview: Dysphasia is a partial language disorder caused by focal damage to the brain's left-hemisphere language network, most commonly from stroke, traumatic brain injury, or brain tumour. By clinical convention, dysphasia denotes an incomplete or milder disruption of language — affecting speaking, comprehension, reading, or writing in any combination — while aphasia refers to the full spectrum including severe loss. In practice, the two terms are used interchangeably in many healthcare systems. Stroke produces dysphasia in approximately 21–38% of acute admissions; partial language recovery begins spontaneously as cerebral oedema resolves and is substantially augmented by early intensive speech-language therapy. Prompt identification is critical because the same brain injury that causes dysphasia is a stroke emergency in which minutes of blood flow restoration determine the extent of permanent language damage.

keyStats.prevalence: Dysphasia/aphasia affects approximately 2 million people in the United States; an estimated 180,000 new cases arise annually, predominantly from stroke (National Aphasia Association, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Highest incidence in adults over 65 following stroke; younger presentations with traumatic brain injury
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of onset around 68–70 when stroke is the cause; can occur at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 5–6 million people worldwide live with some form of post-brain-injury language impairment

definition: Dysphasia (ICD-10: R47.02) is an acquired, partial impairment of language function resulting from focal brain pathology, distinguished from complete aphasia by the preservation of some language capability across at least one modality. The term originates from the Greek 'dys-' (impaired) and '-phasia' (speech), and it is positioned on the same clinical spectrum as aphasia; some national guidelines (e.g., NICE in the UK) use the two interchangeably, while others reserve dysphasia for milder presentations. The underlying mechanism mirrors that of aphasia: disruption to the perisylvian language network in the dominant (usually left) cerebral hemisphere affects word retrieval, grammatical encoding, auditory processing of language, or the phonological and orthographic systems underlying reading and writing. Because language is multifaceted, two people with dysphasia can present very differently: one may struggle mainly to produce words while understanding them well (non-fluent dysphasia), while another produces fluent but error-filled speech with impaired comprehension (fluent dysphasia). The ICD-10 distinguishes dysphasia (R47.02) from aphasia (R47.01) as a coding convenience; clinically, management and rehabilitation principles are identical, and the prognosis is generally more favourable for dysphasia because smaller or less central lesions are typically involved.

typesClassification.description: Reduced speech output, short telegraphic phrases, and word-finding pauses with relatively preserved comprehension; associated with anterior perisylvian (frontal) lesions.
Near-normal speech rate and sentence length but with word substitutions (paraphasias) and impaired comprehension; associated with posterior temporal lesions.
Isolated word-finding difficulty with otherwise fluent and grammatical speech; the mildest and most common residual form after partial recovery.
Disproportionate difficulty with repetition despite fluent output and adequate comprehension, caused by disconnection of anterior and posterior language areas.
Reduced output and impaired comprehension with preserved repetition; caused by watershed zone infarction isolating the perisylvian core.

primarySymptoms: Word-finding pauses — stopping mid-sentence to search for a word that should be automatic, often substituting a related or phonologically similar word instead.
Simplified sentence structure — using shorter, grammatically incomplete phrases when trying to speak; omitting function words such as articles and prepositions.
Phonemic paraphasias — replacing sounds within a word (saying 'cot' instead of 'cat') in a pattern that reflects phonological processing errors.
Semantic paraphasias — substituting a word related in meaning (saying 'chair' instead of 'table') reflecting access errors at the semantic level.
Difficulty following complex spoken instructions or multi-step directions, even when single words are understood.
Impaired repetition — being unable to accurately echo a phrase just heard, even when both comprehension and production appear partially intact.
Reading errors — misreading words aloud, losing place in text, or failing to extract meaning from written sentences (dyslexia as part of the language syndrome).
Writing errors — producing misspelt, incomplete, or semantically substituted words in written output (dysgraphia).
Frustration and reduced conversation initiation resulting from awareness of errors and communication failures.
Variable performance depending on fatigue, stress, and the complexity of the topic, with better performance for emotionally salient or highly practised material.
earlyWarningSigns: Unusual hesitation before answering simple familiar questions.
Using 'thing' or 'it' as a frequent filler instead of specific nouns.
Making uncharacteristic word substitution errors in written messages.
Difficulty following rapid speech or television dialogue that was previously effortless.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of language difficulty — any trouble speaking, understanding, reading, or writing — requires emergency evaluation as a possible stroke (call 999/911 immediately).
Language change accompanied by facial drooping, arm weakness, or a severe sudden headache.
Rapid worsening of dysphasia over minutes or hours in a person known to have a brain tumour or on anticoagulation.
New language difficulty combined with fever and headache, suggesting encephalitis.

causes.description: Left middle cerebral artery territory infarction is the leading cause; even small cortical or subcortical infarcts in strategically located language areas produce dysphasia. Lacunar infarcts in the left internal capsule or basal ganglia cause non-fluent dysphasia with preserved cortical function.

Intracerebral haemorrhage or subarachnoid haemorrhage in the left hemisphere compresses language cortex; as haematoma resolves and oedema clears, partial language recovery often exceeds that seen in equivalently sized ischaemic lesions.

Focal contusion or diffuse axonal injury affecting left-hemisphere language networks produces dysphasia; penetrating injury from gunshot wounds to the temporal or frontal cortex is a classic cause in military populations and after assault.
Slow-growing gliomas in the left perisylvian region are associated with gradual dysphasia that may predate diagnosis by months; the insidious onset contrasts with the sudden onset of vascular causes.

Herpes simplex encephalitis selectively damages temporal and frontal lobes, producing dysphasia as a prominent early feature; autoimmune encephalitides — anti-NMDA receptor, anti-LGI1 — can also impair language networks.

Primary progressive aphasia — a frontotemporal lobar degeneration syndrome — presents as slowly progressive dysphasia, typically affecting word retrieval, motor speech programming, or semantic knowledge before other cognitive domains are involved.

riskFactors.description: Stroke incidence rises steeply with age, and most dysphasia cases follow stroke; the over-65 population carries the highest absolute risk.
Uncontrolled hypertension is responsible for approximately 50% of all strokes; reducing systolic blood pressure by 10 mmHg cuts stroke risk by about 27%.
Cardioembolic strokes from AF preferentially affect large vessel territories including the middle cerebral artery; anticoagulation reduces AF-related stroke by roughly 60%.
Diabetes doubles stroke risk through accelerated atherosclerosis and promotes lacunar infarcts in subcortical language pathways.
Smoking roughly doubles stroke risk; cessation benefits accumulate progressively over 2–5 years.
Recurrent stroke risk is approximately 10% in the first year after a TIA; secondary prevention medications and lifestyle modification substantially reduce this.
Language is processed predominantly in the left hemisphere in about 95% of right-handers; left-sided brain injury of almost any type carries a disproportionate language risk.

riskFactors.factor: Head injury (contact sports, road traffic accidents)
riskFactors.description: Helmet use, seat belts, and safety protocols reduce TBI incidence; persistent post-concussion word-finding difficulty occurs in approximately 10–15% of moderate-to-severe TBI survivors.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 65 following ischaemic or haemorrhagic stroke
Working-age adults (under 50) after traumatic brain injury
Adults aged 50–70 with primary progressive aphasia
People with poorly controlled hypertension, AF, or diabetes
Military veterans and contact-sport athletes with cumulative TBI
Men and women approximately equally affected overall
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of dysphasia relies on clinical language assessment and neuroimaging. The bedside evaluation tests spontaneous speech (fluency, paraphasias, hesitancy), auditory comprehension (following verbal commands of increasing length), repetition, confrontation naming, oral reading, and writing to dictation. Bedside tools such as the Frenchay Aphasia Screening Test or the Mississippi Aphasia Screening Test are used in emergency and ward settings. Standardised assessments — the Western Aphasia Battery or Boston Diagnostic Aphasia Examination — provide severity ratings and subtype classification for rehabilitation planning. In acute presentations, non-contrast CT brain is performed immediately to exclude haemorrhage; MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging detects ischaemic infarct within hours and delineates the lesion with precision sufficient to plan reperfusion intervention. Vascular imaging (CT or MR angiography) identifies large-vessel occlusion. For subacute or progressive presentations, structural MRI assesses for tumour, abscess, or atrophic patterns consistent with frontotemporal degeneration; FDG-PET adds functional localisation when the diagnosis is unclear. Neuropsychological testing separates isolated language impairment from broader cognitive decline.

diagnosticTests.test: Bedside language screen (Frenchay or Mississippi Aphasia Screening Test)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid classification of language deficits at the time of acute presentation to guide triage and immediate management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You will be asked to follow instructions, name objects, repeat sentences, and write a word; the test takes about 5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Western Aphasia Battery (WAB) or Boston Diagnostic Aphasia Examination (BDAE)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides a standardised aphasia quotient score and subtype diagnosis for treatment planning, monitoring, and research.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Administered by a speech-language pathologist over 30–60 minutes covering all language modalities; painless and conversational in style.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging in acute onset to exclude haemorrhage before thrombolysis is considered.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Takes 2–3 minutes; no contrast injection; available in virtually all emergency departments 24 hours a day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines ischaemic infarct size and location; identifies small cortical or subcortical lesions not visible on CT.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Scan takes 20–40 minutes; loud tapping sounds; sometimes contrast is given; no radiation exposure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the site of vessel occlusion to guide thrombectomy planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast dye injection followed by 5–10 minutes of scanning; combined with the initial brain MRI or CT in most stroke protocols.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises the full cognitive profile to distinguish isolated dysphasia from dementia and to quantify strengths for rehabilitation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Conducted by a neuropsychologist over 1–2 hours; involves written and verbal tasks assessing memory, attention, and language in detail.

treatmentOverview: Treatment priority is restoring cerebral perfusion when dysphasia results from acute ischaemic stroke. Intravenous alteplase within 4.5 hours and mechanical thrombectomy up to 24 hours in selected patients are the most effective interventions for limiting brain injury and are the primary reason that sudden dysphasia warrants immediate emergency attendance. Once medically stable, speech-language therapy is the mainstay of rehabilitation. High-intensity therapy — at minimum 3–5 hours per week — produces significantly greater language recovery than low-intensity provision. Specific evidence-based techniques include semantic feature analysis (targeting word retrieval), script training (relearning functional conversational sequences), constraint-induced language therapy (forcing verbal communication), and supported reading programmes for acquired dyslexia. Augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) devices — ranging from picture-based low-tech boards to text-to-speech smartphone applications — support functional communication while verbal skills are rebuilding. Non-invasive brain stimulation (transcranial direct current stimulation or repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation) applied over language cortex shows promise as a therapy adjunct in research settings and specialised centres. Secondary stroke prevention with antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, antihypertensives, and statins addresses the underlying vascular risk. Family and carer education is integral — trained communication partners measurably improve conversation quality and reduce patient isolation.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous thrombolysis (alteplase)
medicalTreatments.description: IV tissue plasminogen activator given within 4.5 hours of ischaemic stroke onset recanalises the occluded artery and limits perilesional damage including to language cortex; eligibility and contraindications are assessed rapidly in the emergency department.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produces an absolute 10–13% increase in independence at 3 months; Number Needed to Treat approximately 10 in eligible patients (NINDS trial, ECASS III).

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical thrombectomy
medicalTreatments.description: Endovascular stent retrieval of large-vessel occlusion, now recommended up to 24 hours post-onset in patients with salvageable brain tissue; can restore perfusion to dysphasic language zones even hours after onset.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: About 46% of patients achieve functional independence versus 26% with medical management alone (HERMES meta-analysis, 2016).

medicalTreatments.name: Intensive speech-language therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Direct drill-based and conversational therapy targeting the specific language modalities impaired; delivered by a qualified speech-language pathologist; intensity of at least 3 hours per week is associated with significantly better outcomes than lower-dose provision.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane meta-analysis (2016) shows statistically and clinically significant improvements in functional communication with higher-dose therapy; approximately 50–60% of patients show meaningful gains with early intensive treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)
medicalTreatments.description: Picture boards, alphabet boards, and smart-device apps providing alternative communication channels when verbal production is severely limited, supporting functional interaction during the recovery phase.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: AAC improves functional communication outcomes in approximately 60–70% of patients with severe language impairment; evidence strongest for low-tech systems and dedicated devices.

medicalTreatments.name: Secondary stroke prevention (antiplatelet/anticoagulant + statin + antihypertensive)
medicalTreatments.description: Combination pharmacotherapy (e.g. aspirin, clopidogrel, statin, ACE inhibitor) reduces risk of recurrent stroke that could worsen or re-cause dysphasia; started within 24–48 hours of non-haemorrhagic stroke.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antiplatelet therapy alone reduces recurrent stroke by approximately 25%; combination secondary prevention reduces 5-year recurrent stroke risk by roughly 50% compared to no treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Melodic intonation therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Uses singing and rhythmic tapping to exploit right-hemisphere musical networks, facilitating verbal output in non-fluent dysphasia; well-studied for Broca's-type presentations.

alternativeTreatments.name: Technology-assisted home therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Tablet and smartphone apps (Tactus Therapy, Constant Therapy, Lingraphica) extend evidence-based practice into daily home sessions; 30–60 minutes of daily self-directed practice supplements clinical therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Communication partner training
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured workshops for family and carers teaching supportive communication strategies; randomised trials show improvements in conversation quality and reductions in carer stress.

whySeeSpecialist: Sudden-onset dysphasia is a stroke emergency that must be assessed by a neurologist within minutes to hours — the window for thrombolysis closes at 4.5 hours. For ongoing management, a speech-language pathologist with specialist experience in neurogenic language disorders delivers rehabilitation. Neuropsychological assessment guides return to work or education and identifies co-existing cognitive deficits. A stroke rehabilitation physician coordinates the multidisciplinary team for inpatient and community recovery. Primary progressive dysphasia warrants referral to a cognitive neurologist or behavioural neurologist experienced in frontotemporal dementia syndromes.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute dysphasia, present immediately to the nearest stroke-capable emergency department. For rehabilitation, seek a speech-language pathologist credentialed in adult neurogenic communication disorders who offers high-frequency therapy and uses standardised outcome measures. Look for a service connected to a multidisciplinary stroke rehabilitation team with neuropsychology, occupational therapy, and social work. For primary progressive aphasia, a specialist dementia or frontotemporal lobar degeneration clinic offers diagnostic certainty and coordinated disease management.
preventionStrategies: Control blood pressure to below 130/80 mmHg — this alone reduces stroke incidence by 35–40%.
Anticoagulate atrial fibrillation appropriately; warfarin or direct oral anticoagulants reduce AF-related embolic stroke by around 60%.
Quit smoking — risk approximates non-smoker levels within 2–5 years of cessation.
Maintain healthy weight and exercise for 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity.
Treat diabetes and hyperlipidaemia with guideline-directed pharmacotherapy.
Act on transient ischaemic attacks immediately — TIA is a warning that requires same-day specialist assessment.
lifestyleModifications: Complete all prescribed speech-language therapy sessions and practise at home daily.
Reduce environmental noise and distractions during conversations to optimise comprehension.
Use a communication notebook, smartphone, or tablet as a bridge when words are inaccessible.
Maintain social contact through aphasia groups, volunteering, and modified recreational activities.
Adopt a Mediterranean or DASH diet to reduce recurrent stroke risk.
Get adequate sleep; fatigue significantly worsens word-finding and comprehension in dysphasia.

recommended: Mediterranean diet pattern — olive oil, vegetables, legumes, fish, nuts — associated with lower stroke incidence and recurrence
Potassium-rich foods (bananas, leafy greens, legumes) supporting blood pressure control
Oily fish twice weekly for omega-3 intake with cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefit
Whole grains and low-glycaemic-index carbohydrates to reduce diabetes risk and glycaemic variability
avoid: High-sodium processed foods and added salt that raise blood pressure
Alcohol above low-risk thresholds (14 units per week UK; 1–2 drinks per day US) which increases stroke risk
Saturated fats and trans fats contributing to atherogenic dyslipidaemia
Erratic dietary vitamin K intake in people anticoagulated with warfarin

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise for at least 150 minutes per week reduces recurrent stroke risk and supports cerebral plasticity that underpins language recovery. Begin with supervised sessions in a stroke rehabilitation programme, adapted to any motor deficits, and progress to independent activity as confidence builds. Group exercise facilitates social interaction that itself provides informal language practice. Resistance training twice weekly complements aerobic activity for overall cardiovascular health.
dailyManagement: Practice recommended speech exercises for 30–60 minutes daily between therapy sessions.
Keep a communication book with key phrases, names, and pictures for situations where words fail.
Take all secondary stroke-prevention medications at consistent times each day.
Use a calendar, alarm, and medication reminder app to compensate for any associated memory difficulty.
Inform healthcare providers about dysphasia so they allow adequate communication time at appointments.
Report any worsening of language function promptly — it may signal a recurrent vascular event.
prognosis: Dysphasia by definition implies incomplete language impairment, and the prognosis is generally more favourable than for severe global aphasia. Approximately 50–65% of patients with post-stroke dysphasia show clinically meaningful improvement at 6 months, with the best recovery in those with small cortical lesions, early therapy initiation, and high therapy intensity. Anomic dysphasia has a particularly good prognosis, with many patients returning to near-normal communication. Non-fluent and fluent subtypes with larger lesions have more variable outcomes. Recovery continues for up to 2 years, and structured therapy produces further gains even in the chronic phase. Primary progressive dysphasia has a more guarded prognosis because deterioration is the underlying biological trajectory; compensatory strategy training delays functional communication breakdown. Depression complicates dysphasia in approximately 25–35% of cases and, when treated, improves both language rehabilitation engagement and quality of life.
recoveryTimeline: The fastest spontaneous recovery occurs in the first 4–6 weeks as oedema and diaschisis resolve. Therapy-driven gains predominate from week 6 through 6–12 months. Many patients plateau at a residual deficit — most commonly mild anomia — by 12 months; however, intensive therapy applied even at 1–2 years post-stroke continues to produce measurable word-retrieval gains. Realistic goals should address functional communication rather than grammatical perfection; returning to work or to valued activities is achievable for many with mild-to-moderate dysphasia.
complications: Persistent residual language impairment in approximately 30–50% of post-stroke cases, most often anomia or word-retrieval latency.
Depression and anxiety, affecting roughly 25–35% of people with dysphasia due to communication barriers and loss of role.
Social withdrawal and isolation resulting from frustration with communication failures and others' misinterpretation of the condition.
Difficulty accessing healthcare — verbal and reading impairments may prevent independent use of appointment systems, medication labels, or written health information.
Unemployment and reduced income — return to previous employment is achieved by fewer than 40% of working-age survivors of aphasia/dysphasia.
Caregiver stress — family members who act as communication intermediaries often experience elevated psychological burden without adequate professional support.

supportResources.name: National Aphasia Association
supportResources.description: Provides information, community support groups, and a therapist referral directory for people with aphasia and dysphasia in the United States.

supportResources.name: Connect — the communication disability network (UK)
supportResources.description: UK resources for people with stroke-related communication disorders including dysphasia support groups and telephone befriending.

supportResources.name: NIDCD — Aphasia information
supportResources.description: Clear overview of language disorders following brain injury from the U.S. National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders.

sources.title: Brady MC et al. Speech and language therapy for aphasia following stroke. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2016
Godefroy O et al. Dysphasia: clinical and anatomical correlations. Rev Neurol (Paris), 2020
NICE NG128 — Stroke and transient ischaemic attack in over 16s: diagnosis and initial management, 2019
Berthier ML. Poststroke aphasia: epidemiology, pathophysiology and treatment. Drugs Aging 2005
Sacco RL et al. Guidelines for Prevention of Stroke: AHA/ASA 2006
Robey RR. A meta-analysis of clinical outcomes in the treatment of aphasia. J Speech Lang Hear Res 1998

confusedWithConditions.name: Aphasia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aphasia and dysphasia describe the same disorder; aphasia covers the full severity spectrum while dysphasia by convention implies a milder or partial language deficit. The clinical management and rehabilitation are identical.

confusedWithConditions.name: Apraxia of speech
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Apraxia is a motor programming disorder producing inconsistent sound errors and groping articulatory movements; dysphasia is a language disorder. Both may follow left frontal stroke but require different therapeutic approaches.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dementia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dementia causes global cognitive decline across memory, attention, and language; dysphasia is a selective language impairment without primary deficits in other cognitive domains, at least at onset.

relatedConditions.name: Aphasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Dysphasia is a milder form on the same clinical spectrum as aphasia; they share aetiology, assessment tools, and rehabilitation approaches.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Stroke is the leading cause of dysphasia; secondary prevention and vascular risk management are central to preventing further language damage.

relatedConditions.name: Apraxia
relatedConditions.relevance: Apraxia of speech frequently co-occurs with non-fluent dysphasia after left frontal stroke; combined speech-language therapy addresses both.

relatedConditions.name: Alzheimer's disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Word-finding difficulty and dysphasic errors are early features of Alzheimer's disease, necessitating differentiation from post-stroke dysphasia in older patients.

faqs.question: What is dysphasia?
faqs.answer: Dysphasia is a partial language disorder caused by brain damage — most often stroke — that impairs speaking, understanding, reading, or writing while leaving intelligence intact. It is the same disorder as aphasia; the term dysphasia conventionally implies milder or incomplete language impairment.

faqs.question: What causes dysphasia?
faqs.answer: Stroke causes roughly 80% of cases by damaging the left brain's language areas. Traumatic brain injury, brain tumour, and neurological infections account for most other cases. Gradual onset over months suggests primary progressive aphasia rather than a vascular event.

faqs.question: Is dysphasia the same as aphasia?
faqs.answer: Clinically yes — both describe acquired language impairment from brain damage. By convention dysphasia implies a milder or partial deficit and aphasia a more severe one, but many guidelines and clinical teams use the terms interchangeably. Management is identical.

faqs.question: Can dysphasia be cured?
faqs.answer: Many people with dysphasia make substantial language recovery, especially with early intensive speech-language therapy. Around 50–65% of post-stroke cases show clinically meaningful improvement. Mild anomic dysphasia often resolves to near-normal function. Primary progressive dysphasia does not have a curative treatment.

faqs.question: What does dysphasia feel like?
faqs.answer: People describe knowing what they want to say but being unable to retrieve the word, producing the wrong sound or word by mistake, having difficulty following fast speech, and feeling frustrated by the gap between their thoughts and what they can express. Fatigue dramatically worsens all language tasks.

faqs.question: How is dysphasia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: a clinician or speech-language pathologist assesses speaking, comprehension, repetition, naming, reading, and writing. Brain MRI or CT confirms the lesion. Sudden-onset dysphasia is treated as a stroke emergency requiring immediate imaging.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for dysphasia?
faqs.answer: For acute stroke-related dysphasia, thrombolysis within 4.5 hours or thrombectomy within 24 hours restores blood flow. Once medically stable, intensive speech-language therapy — at least 3 hours per week — is the main treatment. Communication aids support daily function during recovery.

faqs.question: How long does dysphasia last?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous improvement is fastest in the first 3 months. Therapy-driven recovery continues through the first year and beyond. Many people reach a stable level of residual mild language difficulty — most often word-finding pauses — by 12 months, though further gains are possible with continued therapy.

faqs.question: Does dysphasia affect intelligence?
faqs.answer: No. Dysphasia impairs only the language system. Intelligence, long-term memory, and personality remain intact. The communication barrier can lead others to incorrectly assume cognitive decline, which is a major source of distress for people with dysphasia.

faqs.question: What is the difference between dysphasia and dysarthria?
faqs.answer: Dysphasia is a language disorder affecting word choice and sentence structure. Dysarthria is a motor speech disorder affecting muscle coordination for clear articulation. In dysarthria the right words are chosen but speech sounds unclear; in dysphasia speech may sound normal but contains incorrect words.

faqs.question: Can stress make dysphasia worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anxiety, fatigue, and pressure consistently worsen word-retrieval and fluency in dysphasia. Many people speak more fluently in familiar, low-stress environments and struggle more in formal or high-stakes situations. Managing stress and fatigue is a practical part of daily communication management.

faqs.question: Is dysphasia a form of dementia?
faqs.answer: Not usually. Post-stroke or post-injury dysphasia is a focal language impairment without broader cognitive decline. However, primary progressive aphasia — a neurodegenerative cause of gradual dysphasia — is related to frontotemporal dementia or Alzheimer pathology and does progress to involve other cognitive domains.

faqs.question: Can children develop dysphasia?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it is uncommon. Children can develop dysphasia after stroke, brain tumour, or encephalitis. Outcomes are often better than in adults because the developing brain has greater plasticity and can reorganise language function to the opposite hemisphere.

faqs.question: How can I communicate better with someone who has dysphasia?
faqs.answer: Speak in shorter sentences at a moderate pace. Give the person time to respond without finishing their words. Ask yes/no questions when open questions are too difficult. Use gesture, writing, and pictures to support comprehension. Avoid busy noisy environments.

faqs.question: What is expressive dysphasia?
faqs.answer: Expressive dysphasia (non-fluent type) refers to difficulty producing spoken language — halting speech, short phrases, word-finding gaps — while comprehension is relatively preserved. It typically results from a lesion in the left frontal (Broca's) area.

faqs.question: What is receptive dysphasia?
faqs.answer: Receptive dysphasia (fluent type) refers to impaired comprehension of spoken language with relatively preserved fluent speech output that may contain word errors. It typically results from lesion in the left posterior temporal (Wernicke's) area.

faqs.question: Should I go to A&E if dysphasia starts suddenly?
faqs.answer: Yes, immediately. Sudden-onset dysphasia is a potential stroke requiring emergency brain imaging and possible thrombolysis within 4.5 hours of onset. Do not wait to see if it improves — call 999 or 911 straight away.

faqs.question: Are there apps that help with dysphasia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Apps such as Tactus Therapy, Constant Therapy, and Lingraphica offer structured language exercises for home practice. AAC apps like Proloquo2Go generate speech from picture symbols for people with severe expressive difficulties. These supplement but do not replace clinical therapy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between dysphasia and dyslexia?
faqs.answer: Dyslexia is a developmental reading disorder arising from differences in phonological processing, present since childhood. Acquired dyslexia in dysphasia develops after brain injury in adults who previously had normal reading. The rehabilitation approaches are different, and acquired reading impairment is addressed directly in aphasia therapy.

faqs.question: Does medication help dysphasia?
faqs.answer: No medication is currently approved specifically for dysphasia. Secondary stroke prevention medications — antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives — reduce the risk of a further stroke causing additional language damage. Some research has examined memantine and piracetam as adjuncts to therapy, but evidence is insufficient to recommend them routinely.

metaDescription: Dysphasia is partial language impairment from brain damage, causing speech, comprehension or reading difficulty. Learn causes, therapy and recovery. Doctor-reviewed.
